confirmed.proposed_fix: Re-tier to B/C (self-reflection, not a validated instrument); the dimensions are internally sensible so no structural fix needed if downgraded.

confirmed.framework: John Alan Lee's love styles / Hendrick & Hendrick Love Attitudes Scale (LAS)
confirmed.official_structure: 6 love styles: Eros, Ludus, Storge, Pragma, Mania, Agape (3 primary + 3 secondary). The validated Love Attitudes Scale measures exactly these six.
confirmed.discrepancy: Adds a 7th dimension, 'Philautia' (self-love), which is NOT part of Lee's typology or the Love Attitudes Scale. Philautia belongs to the popular ancient-Greek 'types of love' listicle framing, not the scientific instrument. The title '7 Love Styles' itself signals the invented extra factor.
confirmed.proposed_fix: Remove the 'philautia' dimension and its 2 items; Lee's Love Attitudes Scale = 6 styles (eros, ludus, storge, pragma, mania, agape). Retitle to '6 Love Styles Test'.

unverified.framework: Social skills inventory (no single canonical instrument; cf. Riggio SSI's 6 factors)
unverified.official_structure: There is no one authoritative 5-factor social-skills scale; the validated Riggio Social Skills Inventory uses 6 different factors (emotional/social expressivity, sensitivity, control), which these five (listening/expression/empathy/boundaries/confidence) do not map to
unverified.discrepancy: Tier A implies a validated factor structure, but the five dimensions are a bespoke competency list, not a published instrument's structure
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify to Tier B/C or frame as a self-assessment of communication competencies; no single canonical structure exists to align to

unverified.framework: Goleman's Six Emotional Leadership Styles (HBR 2000)
unverified.official_structure: 6 styles: Visionary/Authoritative, Coaching, Affiliative, Democratic, Pacesetting, Commanding/Coercive
unverified.discrepancy: 4 of 5 dims (Visionary, Coach, Democratic, Pacesetter) are verbatim Goleman styles, so the test is clearly Goleman-derived, but 'Operator' is not a Goleman style, and two canonical styles (Affiliative, Commanding/Coercive) are missing. Shape is wrong relative to the framework it borrows from.
unverified.proposed_fix: Replace invented 'operator' and add the two missing Goleman styles so dims = visionary, coaching, affiliative, democratic, pacesetting, commanding (6 total); or explicitly rebrand as a non-Goleman generic model.

unverified.framework: Personal/human values (nearest scientific instrument: Schwartz Theory of Basic Human Values)
unverified.official_structure: Schwartz = 10 basic values (self-direction, stimulation, hedonism, achievement, power, security, conformity, tradition, benevolence, universalism) on a circumplex; refined version = 19
unverified.discrepancy: Labeled Tier A but the 5 dims (freedom, security, growth, connection, impact) do not correspond to Schwartz's 10-value structure or any single validated values instrument. Freedom~self-direction, security~security, connection~benevolence loosely map, but growth and impact are not Schwartz values and there is no authoritative public 5-value scheme this matches.
unverified.proposed_fix: Downgrade to Tier B/C as a generic values quiz, OR realign to a published structure (e.g., Schwartz's higher-order 4 or basic 10) to justify Tier A.

unverified.framework: Jungian cognitive functions (MBTI 8-function model)
unverified.official_structure: 8 functions: Ni, Ne, Si, Se, Ti, Te, Fi, Fe (each of 4 functions in an introverted and extraverted attitude)
unverified.discrepancy: Only 6 of the 8 functions are present. Te (Extraverted Thinking) and Fi (Introverted Feeling) are entirely missing, leaving the judging axis asymmetric (has Ti and Fe but not their counterparts).
unverified.proposed_fix: Add 'te' and 'fi' dimensions with 2 balanced items each so all 8 Jungian functions are covered.

unverified.framework: Sensory Processing Sensitivity / Highly Sensitive Person (Aron)
unverified.official_structure: Aron's DOES = 4 components: Depth of processing, Overstimulation, Emotional reactivity/empathy, Sensing the subtle (sensory). (Psychometric HSPS = 3 factors: EOE, LST, AES.)
unverified.discrepancy: The three dims (sensory, emotional, overstimulation) map cleanly onto 3 of Aron's 4 DOES components (Sensing subtle, Emotional reactivity, Overstimulation) but omit 'Depth of processing', the core defining component of the HSP construct.
unverified.proposed_fix: Add a 'Depth of processing' dimension (reflect before acting, notice patterns, deep reflection) with 2-3 items to complete Aron's DOES structure.

unverified.framework: Assertiveness (Rathus Assertiveness Schedule and related scales)
unverified.official_structure: No authoritative consensus factor structure. RAS was conceived as unidimensional; factor-analytic studies yield inconsistent, situation-specific multi-factor solutions (e.g. 8-9 factors, or expressing/confronting/demanding). No established directness/boundaries/receptivity 3-factor model exists.
unverified.discrepancy: The 3 dims are a reasonable conceptual carve-up but do not correspond to any published, validated assertiveness factor structure.

unverified.framework: Life Orientation Test-Revised (LOT-R), Scheier, Carver & Bridges 1994
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Optimism (positive future expectancies) and Pessimism (negative outcome expectancies), consistently supported as a bidimensional (negatively-correlated) structure.
unverified.discrepancy: Adds an invented third 'realism' factor that is not part of the LOT-R structure; the instrument has only Optimism and Pessimism.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the 'realism' dimension; keep Optimism + Pessimism (2 factors). Optionally reverse-key pessimism items to score a single optimism composite as LOT-R allows.

unverified.framework: Multidimensional perfectionism / two-factor higher-order model (Frost FMPS, Hewitt-Flett HMPS, APS-R)
unverified.official_structure: Dominant higher-order 2-factor model: Perfectionistic Strivings (high personal standards) and Perfectionistic Concerns (evaluative/self-critical concerns). No recognized standalone 'Control' factor across FMPS/HMPS/APS-R.
unverified.discrepancy: 'standards' maps to Perfectionistic Strivings and 'self-criticism' to Perfectionistic Concerns, but 'control' is an invented dimension with no counterpart in the established perfectionism factor structures.
unverified.proposed_fix: Drop the 'control' dimension (or fold its items into concerns); align to the 2-factor model = strivings (standards) + concerns (self-criticism).

unverified.framework: Grit Scale / Short Grit Scale (Duckworth et al. 2007; Duckworth & Quinn 2009)
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Consistency of Interest and Perseverance of Effort (under a higher-order grit factor).
unverified.discrepancy: Uses 3 dims (perseverance, focus, recovery); grit has only 2 factors and 'recovery' is an invented dimension not part of the Grit Scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Merge to 2 factors: map 'focus' → Consistency of Interest, and merge 'perseverance' + 'recovery' → Perseverance of Effort.

unverified.framework: Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSES)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional: designed to measure a single dominant global self-esteem factor (10 items, 5 positively and 5 negatively worded).
unverified.discrepancy: Splits an intentionally unidimensional construct into 3 invented sub-factors (confidence, acceptance, resilience), a wrongly-split unidimensional scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single global self-esteem dimension; include reverse-keyed (negatively-worded) items as RSES does, rather than three subscales.

unverified.framework: Locus of Control (Rotter I-E Scale; Levenson IPC Scale)
unverified.official_structure: Rotter: a single bipolar Internal-External dimension. Levenson: 3 distinct factors = Internal, Powerful Others, Chance. No 'Balanced' factor in either authoritative model.
unverified.discrepancy: 'internal' and 'external' are the two poles of Rotter's single bipolar dimension, and 'balanced' is an invented third factor that matches neither Rotter (1 bipolar) nor Levenson (Internal/Powerful Others/Chance).
unverified.proposed_fix: Use a single bipolar Internal-External scale (Rotter) with external items reverse-keyed, OR adopt Levenson's Internal/Powerful-Others/Chance; remove the invented 'balanced' dimension.

unverified.framework: Jeffrey Hall's Five Flirting Styles (Physical, Traditional, Sincere, Playful, Polite)
unverified.official_structure: 5 styles: Physical, Traditional, Sincere, Playful, Polite
unverified.discrepancy: Only 4 dims; keeps 3 of Hall's exact labels (playful, sincere, polite) but drops 'Physical' and 'Traditional' and invents 'Bold'. Shape does not match the named framework it derives from.
unverified.proposed_fix: Restore Hall's 5 styles: replace 'bold' with 'physical' and add 'traditional' (5th dim); rename to match Physical/Traditional/Sincere/Playful/Polite with 2 balanced items each.

unverified.framework: Belbin Team Roles (the only recognized 'team role' typology)
unverified.official_structure: Belbin defines 9 team roles (Plant, Resource Investigator, Co-ordinator, Shaper, Monitor Evaluator, Teamworker, Implementer, Completer Finisher, Specialist). It is copyrighted/proprietary and, per Belbin, is not a psychometric instrument. No public scientific 5-role team structure exists.
unverified.discrepancy: Marked Tier A (public scientific) but no public scientific team-role instrument exists. The 5 invented roles (Driver, Strategist, Harmonizer, Specialist, Finisher) do not match Belbin's 9; only 'Specialist' and 'Finisher' loosely echo Belbin names. Cannot be validated against any authoritative public factor structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify to Tier B and frame as a Belbin-inspired approximation; do not present as a validated scientific instrument.

unverified.framework: None — no validated scientific entrepreneur typology
unverified.official_structure: There is no peer-reviewed public instrument that types founders into Visionary/Operator/Seller/Product. Pop frameworks exist (e.g., Gallup BP10) but none matches this 4-type structure or is public-scientific.
unverified.discrepancy: Marked Tier A but represents no authoritative public scientific framework; the 4 types are a reasonable but invented business taxonomy that cannot be verified against any published factor structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify to Tier B/C (entertainment/self-reflection) so it is not held to a scientific factor-structure bar.

unverified.framework: Listening Styles Profile (LSP-16, Watson/Barker/Weaver)
unverified.official_structure: 4 listening styles: People-oriented, Action-oriented, Content-oriented, Time-oriented.
unverified.discrepancy: The 4th dimension is wrong: the test uses 'Big Picture' (invented) in place of the established 'Time-oriented' style. People/Action/Content are correct; Time is missing.
unverified.proposed_fix: Rename dim 'bigPicture' → 'time' (Time-oriented) and reframe its two items around listening efficiency / wanting concise, to-the-point information.

unverified.framework: Baumrind parenting styles
unverified.official_structure: 4 styles defined by responsiveness x demandingness: Authoritative, Authoritarian, Permissive, Uninvolved (Neglectful).
unverified.discrepancy: The 4 dimensions (Nurturing, Structured, Autonomy, Protective) do not correspond to Baumrind's 4 named styles; they read as caregiving-value traits rather than the recognized style typology the 'parenting style' framework is known for.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either map dimensions to Baumrind's 4 styles (Authoritative/Authoritarian/Permissive/Uninvolved), or model the 2 underlying axes (responsiveness/warmth + demandingness/structure) and derive the 4 quadrants.

unverified.framework: Risk tolerance / risk propensity (DOSPERT-style domain models)
unverified.official_structure: Validated risk-taking is either treated as a domain-specific construct (DOSPERT: 5 content domains — ethical, financial, health/safety, recreational, social) or as a single risk-propensity continuum. No validated instrument uses Bold/Calculated/Cautious as three orthogonal factors.
unverified.discrepancy: Bold and Cautious are opposite poles of a single risk-appetite continuum (strongly negatively correlated), so scoring them as separate dimensions splits one unidimensional scale into pseudo-factors; 'Calculated' is a blend rather than an independent factor. Does not match any validated structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either score as one unidimensional risk-tolerance scale (make 'cautious' items reverse-keyed toward a single trait), or adopt DOSPERT's 5 content domains as the dimensions.

unverified.framework: Type A / Type B behavior pattern (Friedman & Rosenman)
unverified.official_structure: Type A and Type B are the two opposite poles of a SINGLE bipolar continuum, not independent factors. There is no third 'balanced' factor — balance is simply the midpoint of the one continuum.
unverified.discrepancy: Splits one bipolar continuum into two independent scales (typeA, typeB) and invents a third scored dimension 'balanced' that has no basis in the framework.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the invented 'balanced' dimension; score as a single A<->B continuum by reverse-keying the Type B items into a Type A scale (or keep typeA/typeB explicitly as two poles of one axis).

unverified.framework: Type D (distressed) personality, DS14 scale (Denollet)
unverified.official_structure: DS14 = exactly 2 factors: Negative Affectivity (7 items) and Social Inhibition (7 items). Type D = high on both. There is no 'resilience' factor.
unverified.discrepancy: Adds an invented third dimension 'resilience' (coping/self-soothing) that is not part of the DS14 construct.
unverified.proposed_fix: Drop the 'resilience' dimension; keep only negativeAffect (Negative Affectivity) and inhibition (Social Inhibition) — official DS14 = 2 factors.

unverified.framework: Burnout Clinical Subtypes (BCSQ, Montero-Marin & Garcia-Campayo)
unverified.official_structure: BCSQ recognizes exactly 3 burnout subtypes: Frenetic (overload), Underchallenged, and Worn-out (neglect/lack of acknowledgment/control). 'Values mismatch' belongs to Maslach's Areas of Worklife model, not the subtype framework.
unverified.discrepancy: overload/underchallenged/neglect map cleanly to the 3 BCSQ subtypes, but a 4th dimension 'valuesMismatch' is imported from a different model, giving 4 factors where the subtype framework has 3.
unverified.proposed_fix: Drop 'valuesMismatch' (or fold it into 'neglect'/worn-out); recognized burnout-subtype model = 3 subtypes (frenetic, underchallenged, worn-out).

unverified.framework: Belbin Team Roles
unverified.official_structure: Belbin = 9 team roles: Plant, Resource Investigator, Co-ordinator, Shaper, Monitor Evaluator, Teamworker, Implementer, Completer Finisher, Specialist.
unverified.discrepancy: Only 6 of the 9 roles are present. Missing three real roles: Resource Investigator, Monitor Evaluator, and Specialist.
unverified.proposed_fix: Add the 3 missing roles with 2 balanced items each: resourceInvestigator (networking/exploring opportunities), monitorEvaluator (impartial/critical judgement), specialist (deep single-subject expertise). Belbin = 9 roles.

unverified.framework: Generic 'Resilience' (no single canonical instrument; e.g. BRS, CD-RISC, Wagnild & Young RS)
unverified.official_structure: No single authoritative factor structure: BRS is unidimensional, CD-RISC's factor structure is unstable, RS uses different factors; the test names no specific instrument
unverified.discrepancy: Dimensions (Recovery, Adaptability, Support, Meaning) are reasonable and reflect common resilience frameworks, but there is no one published structure to validate against.

unverified.framework: Adaptability (individual differences in adaptability)
unverified.official_structure: No single dominant public-domain instrument. Martin et al. Adaptability Scale = unidimensional or 2 lower-order factors (cognitive-behavioral, affective); Ployhart & Bliese I-ADAPT = 8 dimensions (crisis, work stress, creativity, uncertainty, learning, interpersonal, cultural, physical).
unverified.discrepancy: The 4 factors (flexibility, learning, stability, resourcefulness) do not match any established public structure: not Martin's 2/1-factor model nor I-ADAPT's 8 dimensions. 'Stability' is arguably off-construct (steadiness/keeping routines intact is closer to the opposite of adaptability). Appears to be an invented four-factor scheme.
unverified.proposed_fix: No canonical public structure to match. Either reclassify to Tier B/C, or anchor to a named model (e.g. I-ADAPT's dimensions or Martin's cognitive-behavioral/affective split) and drop the off-construct 'stability' factor.

unverified.framework: Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ, Gross & John 2003)
unverified.official_structure: ERQ = 2 factors: Cognitive Reappraisal (6 items) and Expressive Suppression (4 items).
unverified.discrepancy: Test has 4 dimensions (awareness, reappraisal, expression, suppression). Reappraisal and suppression match ERQ, but 'awareness' and 'expression' are not ERQ subscales, and the 4-factor set matches no single canonical instrument exactly (ERQ has 2; the DERS awareness/clarity model has 6). Extra factors are invented relative to the named instrument.
unverified.proposed_fix: Align to ERQ's 2 factors (cognitive reappraisal, expressive suppression); remove the invented 'awareness' and 'expression' dimensions, or explicitly reframe the test as a broader multi-model composite rather than a single instrument.

unverified.framework: Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ, Baer et al. 2006)
unverified.official_structure: FFMQ = 5 facets: Observing, Describing, Acting with Awareness, Non-Judging of inner experience, Non-Reactivity to inner experience.
unverified.discrepancy: Test has only 4 dimensions (observing, describing, nonreactivity, acceptance). Three map to FFMQ facets, but two real facets are missing (Acting with Awareness, Non-Judging), and 'acceptance' is not an FFMQ facet (invented, though conceptually adjacent to non-judging).
unverified.proposed_fix: Restore the 5 FFMQ facets: keep observing/describing/non-reactivity, add acting-with-awareness and non-judging, and either drop 'acceptance' or relabel it as non-judging.

unverified.framework: Toronto Alexithymia Scale (TAS-20)
unverified.official_structure: TAS-20 = 3 factors: Difficulty Identifying Feelings (DIF), Difficulty Describing Feelings (DDF), Externally-Oriented Thinking (EOT).
unverified.discrepancy: Test has 4 dimensions (identifying, describing, external, clarity). The first three map to DIF/DDF/EOT, but 'clarity' is an invented 4th factor not in TAS-20 (its items are essentially reverse-keyed restatements of identifying/describing, adding a redundant non-existent scale).
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the invented 'clarity' dimension; keep the 3 TAS-20 factors (difficulty identifying, difficulty describing, externally-oriented thinking). If reverse-keyed 'clarity' items are wanted, fold them into DIF/DDF as reverse items rather than a separate factor.

unverified.framework: Machiavellianism (Christie & Geis MACH-IV; Dark Triad trait)
unverified.official_structure: The MACH-IV is scored unidimensionally (single Machiavellianism score). Empirical factor analyses yield at most 2 factors: Machiavellian tactics and Machiavellian (cynical) views. As a Dark Triad trait it is treated as one dimension.
unverified.discrepancy: Splits Machiavellianism into 3 invented facets (strategy, guardedness, instrumental) that match neither the unidimensional MACH-IV scoring nor the empirical 2-factor tactics/views structure. This is a wrongly-split unidimensional scale with fabricated factor names.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse into a single unidimensional 'Machiavellianism' scale, or use the empirical 2 factors 'Machiavellian tactics' + 'cynical views'; drop the invented strategy/guardedness/instrumental split.

unverified.framework: Narcissistic Personality Inventory (NPI)
unverified.official_structure: Best-supported NPI structure is 3 factors: Leadership/Authority, Grandiose Exhibitionism, Entitlement/Exploitativeness (Ackerman et al.); also used unidimensionally.
unverified.discrepancy: Dimensions are confidence, admiration, status, humility. 'Humility' is not a narcissism factor at all — it is the opposite pole (cf. HEXACO Honesty-Humility) and cannot be a positive narcissism subscale. The other three are loose relabelings that do not map onto the recognized NPI factors.
unverified.proposed_fix: Replace with the NPI 3-factor structure (Leadership/Authority, Grandiose Exhibitionism, Entitlement/Exploitativeness); remove 'humility' as a narcissism dimension (fold it in only as reverse-keyed items within a factor).

unverified.framework: The Political Compass
unverified.official_structure: Defining shape is 2 bipolar axes forming 4 quadrants: economic (left ↔ right) and social (authoritarian ↔ libertarian).
unverified.discrepancy: Uses 5 unipolar value scales (authority, liberty, equality, tradition, change) instead of the 2 bipolar axes. This cannot place a user in the compass's 4 quadrants, so it fails to reproduce the framework's known shape. (Framing via '-Style' is honest, so no FRAMING_ISSUE.)
unverified.proposed_fix: Restructure into 2 bipolar axes: economic (equality/collective ↔ market/right) and social (authority/authoritarian ↔ liberty/libertarian); fold tradition/change into the social axis rather than as separate dimensions.

unverified.framework: Eysenck PEN model (Psychoticism-Extraversion-Neuroticism), measured by EPQ/EPQ-R
unverified.official_structure: 3 orthogonal superfactors: Psychoticism, Extraversion, Neuroticism (the 'PEN' in the name). The Lie/social-desirability scale is a validity scale, not a personality dimension.
unverified.discrepancy: Test lists 4 dimensions and does not include a proper Psychoticism factor. Extraversion and Emotionality (=Neuroticism) are fine, but Psychoticism is split/renamed into two invented factors 'Tough-Minded' and 'Restraint'. Restraint (impulse control / social appropriateness) is not a PEN superfactor. The model is literally named for its 3 factors, so a 4-factor layout that omits Psychoticism contradicts the framework.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to 3 dimensions P/E/N: merge 'toughMinded' + 'restraint' into a single 'psychoticism' dimension (tough-minded/impulsive/nonconforming items), keep 'extraversion' and rename 'emotionality' to 'neuroticism'.

unverified.framework: 12 Jungian Archetypes (popularized by Mark & Pearson, 'The Hero and the Outlaw')
unverified.official_structure: The canonical pop framework is 12 archetypes: Innocent, Everyman, Hero, Outlaw, Explorer, Creator, Ruler, Magician, Lover, Caregiver, Jester, Sage.
unverified.discrepancy: Only 6 of the 12 archetypes are present (Innocent, Explorer, Sage, Hero, Caregiver, Creator). Missing: Everyman, Outlaw, Ruler, Magician, Lover, Jester. The pop framework is defined by its set of 12; a 6-archetype subset is not faithful to it.
unverified.proposed_fix: Add the 6 missing archetypes (Everyman, Outlaw, Ruler, Magician, Lover, Jester) each with 2 items, for the full set of 12.

unverified.framework: Work values (no single named instrument; closest public structures are O*NET Work Values and Super's Work Values Inventory)
unverified.official_structure: O*NET defines 6 work values: Achievement, Independence(Autonomy), Recognition, Relationships, Support, Working Conditions. Super's WVI uses a different set. The test does not claim a specific instrument.
unverified.discrepancy: The 6 dimensions (autonomy, security, mastery, impact, recognition, belonging) are a generic composite. Autonomy, recognition, and belonging(≈relationships) overlap O*NET, but security, mastery, and impact do not map to any single published work-values structure, and the title claims no specific instrument. No authoritative public factor structure to check exactly against.
unverified.proposed_fix: If alignment to a real instrument is desired, adopt the O*NET 6 (Achievement, Independence, Recognition, Relationships, Support, Working Conditions); otherwise leave as a generic composite (acceptable but unverifiable).

unverified.framework: Hogan Development Survey (HDS) dark-side derailers
unverified.official_structure: HDS = 11 proprietary derailer scales (Excitable, Skeptical, Cautious, Reserved, Leisurely, Bold, Mischievous, Colorful, Imaginative, Diligent, Dutiful); no public Tier-A leadership-derailer instrument has a 5-factor structure
unverified.discrepancy: Only 5 dims. Bold/Diligent/Dutiful are verbatim HDS scales while Volatile approx Excitable and Distant approx Reserved are renamed, so this is a reduced Hogan-style (proprietary, 11-scale) test mislabeled as a Tier-A public instrument; it matches no published public factor structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify as Tier B 'Hogan-style' with honest inspired-by framing; optionally restore true HDS names (Excitable, Reserved). Do not present as an exact public/scientific instrument.

unverified.framework: Winnie Dunn's Model of Sensory Processing (Sensory Profile)
unverified.official_structure: 4 quadrants: Low Registration (Bystander), Sensation Seeking, Sensory Sensitivity, Sensation Avoiding
unverified.discrepancy: 3 dims map correctly (Sensitive=Sensitivity, Seeking, Avoiding) but the 4th dim 'Balanced' is invented and replaces Dunn's 'Low Registration' quadrant, distorting the model's four-quadrant shape.
unverified.proposed_fix: Rename dim 'balanced' to 'registration' (Low Registration / Bystander) and reword its two items toward missing/under-registering sensory input, completing Dunn's 4 quadrants.

unverified.framework: Jealousy typology (reactive/anxious/possessive) / Multidimensional Jealousy Scale
unverified.official_structure: Recognized structures: 3 jealousy types (reactive, anxious/suspicious, possessive) or MJS 3 dimensions (cognitive, emotional, behavioral)
unverified.discrepancy: The 4 dims (Security, Comparison, Boundary, Independent) do not map to any recognized jealousy framework's types or dimensions; they appear invented rather than derived from the reactive/anxious/possessive typology.
unverified.proposed_fix: Align dims to the reactive/anxious(suspicious)/possessive typology (optionally add a secure/trusting style) so the shape matches an established jealousy framework.

unverified.framework: Decision fatigue / ego-depletion (Baumeister); decision-making style scales
unverified.official_structure: Decision fatigue is a documented phenomenon (ego-depletion research) but has no validated multi-factor self-report factor structure; the nearest decision-style instrument is Schwartz's Maximization Scale (maximizing vs satisficing)
unverified.discrepancy: The 4 dims (Overload, Speed, Delegation, Ritual) are invented coping styles with no published/validated factor structure, so a Tier-A 'matches the official factors' claim cannot be substantiated.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify as a Tier B/entertainment coping-style quiz with honest framing; do not present as a validated multi-factor scientific instrument.

unverified.framework: Color personality typology (Insights Discovery / True Colors / Color Code)
unverified.official_structure: All major color-personality systems use 4 colors: Insights Discovery = Red/Blue/Green/Yellow; True Colors = Blue/Gold/Green/Orange; Color Code = Red/Blue/White/Yellow. None includes a 5th 'Purple' color.
unverified.discrepancy: Test uses 5 colors (Red, Blue, Yellow, Green, Purple). The first four map cleanly onto the Insights/True Colors 4-color model, but 'Purple' is an invented fifth color found in no recognized color-personality framework.
unverified.proposed_fix: Drop the 'purple' dimension (and its 2 items) to match the standard 4-color model; or explicitly frame as a custom 5-color variant.

unverified.framework: D&D moral alignment grid
unverified.official_structure: A 3x3 grid of 9 alignments: Lawful/Neutral/Chaotic crossed with Good/Neutral/Evil, yielding Lawful Good, Neutral Good, Chaotic Good, Lawful Neutral, True Neutral, Chaotic Neutral, Lawful Evil, Neutral Evil, Chaotic Evil.
unverified.discrepancy: Only 6 of the 9 alignments are present; the entire Evil row (Lawful Evil, Neutral Evil, Chaotic Evil) is omitted, breaking the defining 3x3 shape.
unverified.proposed_fix: Add Lawful Evil, Neutral Evil, and Chaotic Evil dimensions (2 items each) to complete the canonical 9-alignment grid.

unverified.framework: Hogwarts house sorting (Harry Potter)
unverified.official_structure: 4 houses, each with a defining trait: Gryffindor (courage/bravery), Ravenclaw (wisdom/intelligence), Hufflepuff (loyalty/fairness), Slytherin (ambition/cunning).
unverified.discrepancy: Test has 6 dimensions. The first four (courage, wisdom, loyalty, ambition) map to the 4 houses, but 'creativity' and 'balance' are invented houses with no basis in the canonical 4-house framework.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the 'creativity' and 'balance' dimensions (4 items) to restore the canonical 4-house structure.

unverified.framework: Emotional maturity (nearest validated instrument: Singh & Bhargava Emotional Maturity Scale)
unverified.official_structure: No single consensus factor structure for 'emotional maturity.' The most-cited validated instrument, the Singh & Bhargava EMS, uses 5 factors: Emotional Stability, Emotional Progression, Social Adjustment, Personality Integration, Independence.
unverified.discrepancy: Test's 4 dimensions (awareness, responsibility, regulation, empathy) are an emotional-intelligence-style framing and match neither the validated EMS 5-factor structure nor any other authoritative public model of emotional maturity.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either realign dimensions to the validated EMS 5-factor model, or relabel/reframe as an emotional-intelligence-inspired quiz rather than an 'emotional maturity' measure.

unverified.framework: Self-control (Tangney Brief Self-Control Scale, BSCS)
unverified.official_structure: The BSCS is standardly treated as unidimensional; the best-supported multi-factor model is 2 factors — Restraint and (non-)Impulsivity. It does not contain 'recovery' or 'flexibility' subscales.
unverified.discrepancy: Test invents a 4-facet structure (impulse, planning, recovery, flexibility). Only 'impulse' corresponds to the BSCS; 'recovery' and 'flexibility' are fabricated facets not present in the validated instrument. All items are positively keyed, whereas the BSCS is majority reverse-keyed.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to the BSCS structure: either 1 unidimensional self-control scale or 2 factors (Restraint, Impulsivity); drop 'recovery' and 'flexibility'; add reverse-keyed impulsivity items.

unverified.framework: Relational needs (nearest validated instrument: Relational Needs Satisfaction Scale, Erskine)
unverified.official_structure: No single consensus public structure for 'relationship needs.' The validated RNSS (Erskine) identifies 5 dimensions: authenticity, support and protection, having an impact, shared experience, and initiative from the other.
unverified.discrepancy: Test's 6 dimensions (security, freedom, affection, growth, honesty, support) are a plausible pop conceptualization but do not match the validated RNSS 5-dimension structure or any other authoritative published factor model.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either realign to the validated RNSS 5-dimension model, or reclassify as a Tier B/inspired-by relationship quiz rather than a Tier A scientific instrument.

unverified.framework: Person-organization fit / Organizational Culture Profile (OCP; O'Reilly, Chatman & Caldwell)
unverified.official_structure: The closest authoritative instrument (OCP) uses 7 dimensions: Innovation, Stability, Respect for People, Outcome Orientation, Attention to Detail, Team Orientation, Aggressiveness. There is no single canonical public 'culture fit' factor structure matching these 5 dims (Innovative, Stable, People-Centered, Performance, Autonomy).
unverified.discrepancy: The 5 chosen dims partially echo the OCP (innovation/stability/respect-for-people/outcome) but drop Attention to Detail, Team Orientation, Aggressiveness and add 'Autonomy', which is not an OCP dimension. It does not reproduce any single authoritative published structure, so it cannot be validated as a Tier A scientific instrument.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either reclassify as Tier B (culture-fit approximation, framing must not imply a validated instrument), or align dims to the OCP-7 (Innovation, Stability, Respect for People, Outcome Orientation, Attention to Detail, Team Orientation, Aggressiveness).

unverified.framework: Life purpose / meaning (no authoritative multi-factor scientific instrument for these 6 domains)
unverified.official_structure: Validated meaning/purpose scales (e.g., Meaning in Life Questionnaire) use 2 factors (Presence, Search); no published scientific instrument defines purpose as Service/Mastery/Creation/Connection/Exploration/Stability. This is an invented domain typology.
unverified.discrepancy: The 6 dimensions are a plausible but invented 'sources of purpose' typology with no authoritative published factor structure, so it cannot meet the Tier A bar of matching a validated instrument.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify as Tier B/entertainment approximation; do not present as a validated scientific measure of life purpose.

unverified.framework: Difficult Person Test (IDR Labs), based on Sleep et al. (2019) structure of antagonism
unverified.official_structure: 7 antagonism traits: Callousness, Grandiosity, Aggressiveness, Suspicion, Manipulativeness, Dominance, Risk-taking.
unverified.discrepancy: Only 6 of the 7 canonical traits are present; the 'Manipulativeness' dimension is missing entirely. The other 6 dims match the published structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Add a 'manipulativeness' dimension with ~2 balanced items (e.g., 'You are willing to use charm or leverage to get what you want.'; 'You can adjust how you present things to influence people.') to restore the full 7-factor antagonism structure.

unverified.framework: The Dark Factor of Personality (D-factor; Moshagen, Hilbig & Zettler, 2018)
unverified.official_structure: D is a single common dark core expressed through 9 dark traits: Egoism, Machiavellianism, Moral Disengagement, Narcissism, Psychological Entitlement, Psychopathy, Sadism, Self-interest, Spitefulness.
unverified.discrepancy: 4 of the 5 dims map to real facets (egoism, entitlement=psychological entitlement, moralDisengagement, spite=spitefulness), but 'exploitation' is NOT one of the 9 canonical dark traits — it is an invented factor. Its items ('notice leverage', 'how people can be persuaded') are actually Machiavellianism/manipulation themes.
unverified.proposed_fix: Rename dim 'exploitation' -> 'machiavellianism' (label 'Machiavellianism'); its two items already describe manipulative/strategic leverage. This makes all 5 dims a valid subset of the 9 official dark traits.

unverified.framework: Dweck's Implicit Theories of Intelligence / Mindset Scale (ITIS)
unverified.official_structure: A single bipolar, unidimensional construct: fixed (entity) mindset at one pole and growth (incremental) mindset at the other; fixed items are reverse-keyed growth items and averaged into one score.
unverified.discrepancy: 'growth' and 'fixed' correctly represent the two poles, but 'performance' is a separate construct from the achievement-goal-orientation literature (performance vs. mastery goals), and 'experimental' is invented. The scale has no 'performance' or 'experimental' factor.
unverified.proposed_fix: Drop dims 'performance' and 'experimental'; keep growth vs fixed as a single bipolar scale (make 'fixed' items reverse-keyed toward growth), matching Dweck's unidimensional structure.

unverified.framework: No established 'decision confidence' instrument; closest validated is the General Decision-Making Style Inventory (GDMS)
unverified.official_structure: No authoritative 'decision confidence' factor structure exists. The nearest validated instrument (GDMS) uses entirely different factors: rational, intuitive, dependent, avoidant, spontaneous.
unverified.discrepancy: The dims (clarity, selfTrust, risk, review) are an original, non-published construct with no authoritative structure to check against. Internally coherent and reasonable, but not a recognized instrument.

unverified.framework: Composite of demand/withdraw (pursue-withdraw) pattern + Gottman-style repair; no single published 5-factor instrument
unverified.official_structure: The demand-withdraw (pursue-withdraw) pattern is well documented, and repair attempts are a real Gottman construct, but there is no single validated instrument that partitions conflict into these 5 named dimensions (pursue, withdraw, escalate, appease, repair).
unverified.discrepancy: The 5 dims form a plausible eclectic composite drawn from real constructs, but there is no authoritative public factor structure to verify the exact count/names against.

unverified.framework: People-pleasing / sociotropy (People Pleasing Scale 2026; Sociotropy-Autonomy Scale)
unverified.official_structure: Competing validated structures, both 3-factor: People Pleasing Scale = Responsibility, Neglect of Own Needs, Others' Expectations; SAS sociotropy = Concern About Disapproval, Attachment/Concern About Separation, Pleasing Others. No single canonical structure.
unverified.discrepancy: The dims (approval, overgiving, conflictAvoidance, boundaryGrowth) do not match either validated 3-factor scale, and 'boundaryGrowth' is an aspirational/recovery theme rather than a people-pleasing subscale. However, because the construct has multiple competing structures rather than one authoritative one, it is treated as unverifiable rather than a hard mismatch.
unverified.proposed_fix: Optional: replace 'boundaryGrowth' with a core people-pleasing facet (e.g. 'neglectOfOwnNeeds') to better align with the validated People Pleasing Scale, since a growth/boundary dim is not part of the construct.

unverified.framework: No recognized proprietary 'Control Style' instrument; loosely a therapeutic control/anxiety-coping construct
unverified.official_structure: There is no known proprietary or published framework named 'Control Style' with a defined dimension set. The dims (planning, perfection, relational, surrender) are an original construct.
unverified.discrepancy: As a Tier B '-style' test, framing is honest (the generic 'Style' naming does not claim to be an official instrument), but there is no recognized framework SHAPE to verify against, so structure cannot be confirmed.

unverified.framework: Social confidence / social self-efficacy
unverified.official_structure: No single authoritative public instrument defines a fixed factor structure for 'social confidence'. Related constructs are measured by social self-efficacy scales (e.g., Perceived Social Self-Efficacy) treated largely as unidimensional; the 4 dims (initiation, visibility, recovery, belonging) are a reasonable but invented decomposition with no published factor model to check against.
unverified.discrepancy: Tier-A bar requires matching a published factor structure, but none exists for this construct; dims are constructed, not validated. (Arguably belongs in Tier B.)
unverified.proposed_fix: Either reclassify to Tier B as an inspired-by self-efficacy composite, or leave as-is; no authoritative structure to align to.

unverified.framework: Rejection Sensitivity Questionnaire (Downey & Feldman 1996; Adult A-RSQ, Berenson 2009)
unverified.official_structure: RS is conceptualized and validated as essentially UNIDIMENSIONAL (a single cross-situational factor); the adult A-RSQ scores each scenario on two components, rejection concern (anxiety) and rejection expectancy, combined into one RS composite. There are no 'anticipation / interpretation / recovery / directness' factors.
unverified.discrepancy: The test splits a unidimensional construct into 4 invented factors; 'recovery' and 'directness' actually measure resilient coping (the opposite pole), not rejection sensitivity, so they contaminate the scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Score as one unidimensional RS scale, or use the 2 A-RSQ components (rejection concern + rejection expectancy). Drop/relabel the reverse-valenced coping items ('recovery','directness') so they do not count as RS.

unverified.framework: Achievement motivation (McClelland nAch; Spence & Helmreich Work-and-Family Orientation, WOFO)
unverified.official_structure: McClelland's nAch is a single acquired need. The standard multidimensional public instrument, WOFO (Helmreich & Spence 1978), has 3 factors: Mastery, Work (effort), and Competitiveness (a 4th, Personal Unconcern, is sometimes added). There is no 'progress' factor and no separate 'standards' factor.
unverified.discrepancy: Dims mastery + competition track WOFO, but 'work/effort orientation' is dropped, 'standards' is a mastery duplicate, and 'progress' (small wins/momentum) is invented — so the 4 dims do not match any published structure exactly.
unverified.proposed_fix: Align to WOFO 3 factors: Mastery, Work/Effort, Competitiveness. Fold 'standards' into Mastery and replace 'progress' with a Work/effort dimension.

unverified.framework: McClelland need for power (nPow); Winter power motive
unverified.official_structure: nPow is a single unidimensional acquired need — the drive toward influence, control, status, and impact on others. It is not a 4-factor instrument.
unverified.discrepancy: The test splits the unidimensional power need into 4 factors; 'autonomy' in particular is a Self-Determination-Theory need, not part of power motivation, so it is a wrongly-imported factor.
unverified.proposed_fix: Score as one nPow scale (influence/status/impact facets can remain as items but not as separate validated factors). Remove 'autonomy' as it is not a power-motivation facet.

unverified.framework: McClelland need for affiliation (nAff); cf. Need to Belong (Baumeister & Leary)
unverified.official_structure: nAff is a single unidimensional acquired need — the drive for warm, accepting relationships and harmonious social contact. The related Need-to-Belong scale is also unidimensional. No canonical 4-factor affiliation instrument exists.
unverified.discrepancy: The test splits a unidimensional need into 4 invented factors (belonging/approval/closeness/harmony); 'approval' overlaps with a distinct construct (need for approval), and none are separately validated affiliation factors.
unverified.proposed_fix: Score as one nAff scale; treat belonging/closeness/harmony/approval as item content, not as distinct validated dimensions.

unverified.framework: Achievement Goal Orientation (VandeWalle 1997 work-domain; Elliot & McGregor 2×2)
unverified.official_structure: VandeWalle's validated work-domain instrument has 3 factors: Learning, Prove-Performance, Avoid-Performance. Elliot's 2×2 framework has 4: Mastery-Approach, Mastery-Avoidance, Performance-Approach, Performance-Avoidance. In both, 'learning' and 'mastery' are the SAME construct, and there is no 'contribution' goal orientation.
unverified.discrepancy: The test has 5 dims with a duplicate (learning = mastery) and a fully invented one (contribution); 'performance' + 'avoidance' are not split by approach/avoidance the way the 2×2 requires.
unverified.proposed_fix: Use VandeWalle's 3 factors — merge learning+mastery into 'Learning', keep 'Performance' as Prove, keep 'Avoidance' as Avoid-Performance, and drop 'contribution'. (Or adopt the Elliot 2×2.)

unverified.framework: Authenticity Scale (Wood, Linley, Maltby, Baliousis & Joseph, 2008)
unverified.official_structure: 3 factors: Authentic Living, Accepting External Influence (reverse-keyed), Self-Alienation (reverse-keyed)
unverified.discrepancy: Test uses 4 invented dimensions (Self-Knowledge, Expression, Alignment, Belonging) that do not correspond to the published tripartite structure. 'Belonging' has no basis in the scale, and the two reverse-keyed constructs (Self-Alienation, Accepting External Influence) are absent; all 8 items are positively keyed.
unverified.proposed_fix: Replace the 4 dimensions with the 3 published factors: Authentic Living, Accepting External Influence, Self-Alienation. Key the latter two as reverse (high score = low authenticity) with balanced items each.

unverified.framework: No established scientific 'boundary repair' instrument exists
unverified.official_structure: No authoritative published factor structure. Dimensions (Naming, Asserting, Repair, Follow-through) are a plausible clinical breakdown but not tied to any validated scale.
unverified.discrepancy: Marked Tier A (public scientific instrument) but there is no authoritative public instrument to validate against; it is an original self-help construct.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify to Tier B/C (no published structure to audit against), or frame explicitly as an original, non-validated reflection tool rather than a scientific instrument.

unverified.framework: No public scientific 'conflict trigger' scale; closest is the proprietary Conflict Dynamics Profile 'hot buttons' (Davis)
unverified.official_structure: No authoritative public factor structure. The nearest real instrument, the CDP, uses 9 proprietary 'hot buttons' (Unreliable, Overly-Analytical, Unappreciative, Aloof, Micromanaging, Self-Centered, Abrasive, Untrustworthy, Hostile), not these 5 factors.
unverified.discrepancy: Marked Tier A but the 5 dimensions (Disrespect, Abandonment, Control, Unfairness, Criticism) do not match any published public instrument; they overlap loosely with the proprietary CDP hot buttons but the counts and names differ.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify to Tier B and frame as inspired-by the Conflict Dynamics 'hot buttons' concept rather than a validated public scale, or drop the Tier A scientific claim.

unverified.framework: Self-Monitoring Scale (Snyder 1974; Lennox & Wolfe Revised 1984)
unverified.official_structure: Revised Self-Monitoring Scale = 2 factors: Ability to Modify Self-Presentation, Sensitivity to Expressive Behavior of Others (Snyder's original is unidimensional; Briggs & Cheek find 3).
unverified.discrepancy: Test uses 4 dimensions. Cue Reading maps to Sensitivity and Expression Control/Adaptive to Ability, but the Ability factor is wrongly split into two (Adaptive, Expression Control) and a 'Consistent' factor is invented from low-self-monitoring items. Those 'Consistent' items ('act like the same person everywhere', 'being genuine matters more than fitting in') indicate LOW self-monitoring yet are keyed reverse:false, inverting their contribution.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to the 2 published factors: merge Adaptive + Expression Control into 'Ability to Modify Self-Presentation'; keep Cue Reading as 'Sensitivity to Expressive Behavior of Others'. Either drop the 'Consistent' items or fold them into the Ability factor with reverse:true.

unverified.framework: Emotional Availability (Biringen EA Scales / EA Self-Report)
unverified.official_structure: Scientific EA is a parent-child DYADIC construct: observational EAS = 4 adult dims (sensitivity, structuring, nonintrusiveness, nonhostility) + 2 child dims; EA-SR = 3 dims (close/positive, distant/hard-to-soothe, emotional scaffolding). No validated public self-report of general adult 'emotional availability' with presence/vulnerability/responsiveness/boundaries.
unverified.discrepancy: Tier-A claim, but the four dims (presence, vulnerability, responsiveness, boundaries) do not match any published EA factor structure; the popular 'emotionally available partner' trait has no validated public structure, and the academic EA construct is dyadic parent-child with entirely different factors.
unverified.proposed_fix: Retier to B (proprietary/inspired-by) or C, or relabel; do not present as a validated scientific instrument. If kept as pop trait, frame as an informal self-reflection, not the Biringen EA construct.

unverified.framework: 3-Factor Patience Scale / Three-Factor Patience Questionnaire (Schnitker, 2012)
unverified.official_structure: 3 factors: Interpersonal patience, Life-hardship patience, Daily-hassles patience
unverified.discrepancy: Test uses 4 invented dims (waiting, people, process, frustration). Only 'people' maps to Interpersonal; 'waiting'/'process'/'frustration' do not correspond to the Life-hardship and Daily-hassles factors and split the construct wrongly into an invented 4-factor shape.
unverified.proposed_fix: Restructure to Schnitker's 3 factors: rename to interpersonal-patience (people), life-hardship-patience (process/long timelines), daily-hassles-patience (waiting/delays); fold 'frustration' items into daily-hassles or interpersonal rather than a separate factor.

unverified.framework: Integrity/honesty testing (employment integrity tests; overlaps HEXACO Honesty-Humility)
unverified.official_structure: No single canonical public scientific instrument defines 'integrity' as honesty/consistency/accountability/fairness. Overt/covert integrity tests are proprietary; the nearest validated public factor is HEXACO Honesty-Humility (a single factor with facets Sincerity, Fairness, Greed-Avoidance, Modesty).
unverified.discrepancy: Tier-A claim, but the four dims do not correspond to any published integrity factor structure; 'integrity' has no authoritative public multi-factor self-report to validate against.
unverified.proposed_fix: Retier to B or map explicitly to HEXACO Honesty-Humility facets (sincerity, fairness, greed-avoidance, modesty) if a Tier-A scientific anchor is desired; otherwise frame as an informal self-reflection.

unverified.framework: Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)
unverified.official_structure: No single scale has these 5 factors. Basic Psychological Needs Scale = 3 needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness). The motivation continuum is a separate sub-theory with 6 regulation types (intrinsic, integrated, identified, introjected, external, amotivation).
unverified.discrepancy: Blends two distinct SDT sub-frameworks into one 5-factor scale: the 3 basic needs plus only 2 of the 6 motivation-continuum regulation types (intrinsic, external). This is an invented hybrid; no published instrument uses this structure, and the motivation continuum is grossly truncated.
unverified.proposed_fix: Pick one sub-framework: either restrict to the 3 basic needs (BPNS: autonomy, competence, relatedness) OR represent the motivation continuum honestly (intrinsic + full set of extrinsic regulation types). Do not mix 3 needs with 2 truncated regulation types.

unverified.framework: Regulatory Focus Theory / RFQ (Higgins et al., 2001)
unverified.official_structure: 2 independent factors: Promotion Focus and Prevention Focus. Factor analyses consistently support a clean two-factor structure.
unverified.discrepancy: Adds a third invented dimension 'Flexible' (situational switching between promotion/prevention). Regulatory focus is a two-factor construct; there is no validated 'flexible' factor.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the 'flexible' dimension; keep 2 factors (promotion, prevention), rebalancing its 2 items into the two real subscales.

unverified.framework: Maximization Scale (Schwartz et al., 2002; Nenkov et al. short form)
unverified.official_structure: 3 factors: High Standards, Alternative Search, Decision Difficulty. Maximizing vs satisficing is a single continuum, not two separate factors; regret is a distinct correlated scale, not a maximization subfactor.
unverified.discrepancy: Uses maximizer + satisficer as two separate scored factors (they are poles of one dimension), and includes 'regret' (a separate construct) and 'optionSeeking' (≈ alternative search). Does not map to the canonical 3-factor maximization structure; high standards / decision difficulty are absent.
unverified.proposed_fix: Adopt the 3 canonical maximization factors (high standards, alternative search, decision difficulty); collapse maximizer/satisficer into a single continuum rather than two factors.

unverified.framework: Type C ('cancer-prone') personality (Temoshok; TCPI, Hosaka et al. 1999)
unverified.official_structure: No single canonical factor structure. The TCPI operationalizes 5 subscales (conflict avoidance, emotion suppression, self-sacrifice, lack of expressiveness, despair/helplessness); Type C is otherwise a clinical/theoretical construct without a universally agreed psychometric factor model.
unverified.discrepancy: The 5 dims (restraint, compliance, caution, patience, internalPressure) are thematically faithful to Type C (emotional restraint, compliance/conflict-avoidance, patience/stoicism, internalized pressure) but do not map cleanly to the TCPI's 5 subscales (self-sacrifice and lack-of-expressiveness are absent; 'caution' is not a core Type C facet). No authoritative structure to validate exactly against.
unverified.proposed_fix: Optional: align dims closer to a published operationalization (e.g. TCPI: emotion suppression, conflict avoidance, self-sacrifice, non-expressiveness, helplessness) if a validated structure is desired; otherwise acceptable as a thematic approximation.

unverified.framework: MAPP (Motivational Appraisal of Personal Potential) — Assessment.com
unverified.official_structure: Proprietary commercial instrument; no published public factor model. MAPP organizes results into narrative areas (Interest in Job Contents, Temperament, Aptitude; People/Things/Data worker functions) rather than a validated 6-factor structure.
unverified.discrepancy: Mislabeled Tier A: MAPP is a proprietary, copyrighted commercial test with no peer-reviewed public factor structure, so it cannot be held to the Tier A 'exact published factor structure' bar. The 6 dims (Tasks/People/Things/Ideas/Data/Environment) are a plausible worker-function grouping but do not correspond to any authoritative MAPP factor model.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reclassify to Tier B (proprietary '-style' approximation; title already says 'MAPP-Style'); no factor-structure change required, but treat framing/shape rather than exact factors as the bar.

unverified.framework: Saville Wave Professional/Focus Styles
unverified.official_structure: 4 clusters: Thought, Influence, Adaptability, Delivery (each split into 12 sections / 36 dimensions)
unverified.discrepancy: Test invents a 5th cluster, 'Relationships'. The Wave model has exactly 4 clusters (Thought, Influence, Adaptability, Delivery); relationship/interpersonal content sits within the Influence cluster's sections, not as a separate top-level cluster.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the 'relationships' dimension (fold its two items into 'influence'); Wave = 4 clusters (Thought, Influence, Adaptability, Delivery).

unverified.framework: General Self-Efficacy Scale (Schwarzer & Jerusalem) / New GSE (Chen)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional — a single global self-efficacy factor (all 10 items load on one factor; replicated across 25+ countries)
unverified.discrepancy: A unidimensional construct is wrongly split into 4 invented facets (Problem Solving, Learning, Resilience, Social). GSE has no published sub-scales; these sub-dimensions are fabricated.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse the 4 facets into a single dimension 'general self-efficacy'; keep the 8 items as one balanced scale (unidimensional).

unverified.framework: Self-Concept Clarity Scale (Campbell et al., 1996)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional — a single self-concept-clarity factor (12 items, one dominant factor)
unverified.discrepancy: A unidimensional scale is wrongly split into 4 invented facets (Identity, Values, Preferences, Future). The SCCS has no published sub-scales.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse the 4 facets into a single dimension 'self-concept clarity'; keep items as one unidimensional scale.

unverified.framework: Generic 'cognitive style' (no single named instrument; eclectic composite)
unverified.official_structure: No single authoritative model matches these 6 dims. Established instruments differ: Riding's Cognitive Styles Analysis = 2 dimensions (Wholist-Analytic, Verbal-Imagery); Allinson-Hayes Cognitive Style Index = unidimensional (intuition-analysis); Kirton KAI = adaptor-innovator (unidimensional).
unverified.discrepancy: The 6 dims (Analytical, Intuitive, Verbal, Visual, Practical, Reflective) are a plausible-sounding mashup of dual-process (analytic/intuitive) + Riding verbal/visual + extra pop dimensions, not tied to any one published framework. Title and Popular framing are honest (no false scientific claim), so no framing issue — but there is no authoritative structure to verify against.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either accept as a generic self-reflection quiz (no fix needed given honest framing), or, to anchor to a real model, align to Riding's CSA (2 orthogonal dims: Wholist-Analytic, Verbal-Imagery).

unverified.framework: Rational-Experiential Inventory (REI-40; Epstein & Pacini)
unverified.official_structure: 2 dimensions: Rationality and Experientiality, each with 2 subfacets (Ability, Engagement) = 4 subscales. There is no 'integrative' or 'skeptical' factor.
unverified.discrepancy: Keeps rational + experiential but adds two invented dimensions, 'integrative' and 'skeptical', that do not exist in the REI framework. The framework shape (2 poles / 4 ability-engagement facets) is wrong.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to the REI's 2 dimensions (Rational, Experiential); drop invented 'integrative' and 'skeptical'. Optionally use the 4 authentic subfacets (rational-ability, rational-engagement, experiential-ability, experiential-engagement).

unverified.framework: Empathizing-Systemizing theory (Baron-Cohen; EQ/SQ)
unverified.official_structure: 2 orthogonal dimensions: Empathizing (EQ) and Systemizing (SQ). 'Brain types' (E, S, B, Extreme E, Extreme S) are derived from the two scores, not separately measured factors.
unverified.discrepancy: Adds two invented scored dimensions, 'integrated' and 'contextSwitching', which are not part of the E-S factor structure. The theory has exactly 2 measured axes.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to 2 dimensions (Empathizing, Systemizing); remove 'integrated' and 'contextSwitching'. Any balanced/type label should be derived from the two scores, not scored as its own dimension.

unverified.framework: Experiences in Close Relationships (ECR/ECR-R adult attachment)
unverified.official_structure: 2 orthogonal dimensions: attachment Anxiety and attachment Avoidance. The four styles (Secure, Preoccupied, Dismissing, Fearful) are quadrants derived by crossing the two dimensions, not separate scales.
unverified.discrepancy: Adds 'security' and 'fearful' as separately scored dimensions. These are derived prototypes, not orthogonal factors; the ECR is strictly 2-dimensional.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to 2 dimensions (Anxiety, Avoidance) per ECR; derive Secure (low/low) and Fearful (high/high) as quadrant outcomes from those two scores rather than scoring them as dimensions.

unverified.framework: Dweck implicit theories of intelligence (growth vs fixed mindset)
unverified.official_structure: Dweck's mindset construct = growth vs fixed (a single bipolar implicit-theory dimension, sometimes modeled as 2 correlated factors: Growth, Fixed). Performance vs Mastery goals belong to a separate framework, Achievement Goal Theory, not the mindset instrument.
unverified.discrepancy: Conflates two distinct frameworks: growth/fixed (mindset) are correct, but 'performance' and 'mastery' are achievement-goal orientations imported from a different instrument, presented as if part of the mindset factor structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Restrict to the mindset dimensions (Growth, Fixed); remove 'performance' and 'mastery', or relabel the test as a combined mindset + achievement-goal-orientation measure rather than a pure mindset instrument.

unverified.framework: Personal agency / sense of agency (no single canonical scale)
unverified.official_structure: No authoritative, widely-published factor structure exists for a general 'Personal Agency' instrument; agency is operationalized differently across scales (Bandura self-efficacy, sense-of-agency measures, etc.).
unverified.discrepancy: The five dimensions (ownership, initiative, choice, persistence, support) are internally coherent and balanced, but cannot be validated against a single authoritative published structure.

unverified.framework: Emotional granularity (Feldman Barrett)
unverified.official_structure: Emotional granularity is not measured by a self-report trait factor structure; it is computed as differentiation (e.g., ICC) across repeated emotion ratings. Related self-report scales (RDEES: Range & Differentiation) exist but do not correlate well with actual granularity and have a different structure.
unverified.discrepancy: The five self-report dimensions (labeling, differentiation, bodySignals, expression, usefulness) are invented; there is no authoritative self-report factor model for granularity to validate against.

unverified.framework: Moral Identity Scale (Aquino & Reed, 2002)
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Internalization and Symbolization (respondent rates how central 9 moral traits are to self-concept, and how much they publicly express them)
unverified.discrepancy: The test invents a 5-dimension structure (centrality, care, fairness, loyalty, courage) that does not exist in the Aquino & Reed model. Three of these (care, fairness, loyalty) are actually Moral Foundations Theory factors, not moral-identity dimensions; the real 2-factor Internalization/Symbolization structure is entirely absent.
unverified.proposed_fix: Replace the 5 invented dims with the official 2 factors: 'internalization' (moral traits central to self-concept) and 'symbolization' (public expression of moral traits); ~5 items each.

unverified.framework: Enneagram Three Centers of Intelligence (Triads)
unverified.official_structure: 3 centers: Body/Gut (types 8,9,1), Heart/Feeling (2,3,4), Head/Thinking (5,6,7)
unverified.discrepancy: The three real centers (body, heart, head) are correct, but the test adds a fourth invented dimension, 'integrated,' which is not a center of intelligence in the Enneagram framework. Integration/balance across centers is a general concept, not a measured fourth center.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the invented 'integrated' dimension; official framework = 3 centers only (body, heart, head). Redistribute its items or drop them.

unverified.framework: Ego defense mechanisms (Freud/Vaillant; DSQ traditions)
unverified.official_structure: No single canonical factor structure; the DSQ groups defenses into 3 styles (mature/neurotic/immature) and theorists enumerate widely varying mechanism sets. There is no authoritative 'these 6 defense mechanisms' instrument.
unverified.discrepancy: The 6 chosen mechanisms (rationalization, intellectualization, humor, suppression, projection, acting out) are all genuine defenses, but they are not a published/validated factor structure, so exact fidelity cannot be judged against an authoritative source.

unverified.framework: Attributional Style Questionnaire (Seligman/Peterson), explanatory style
unverified.official_structure: 3 bipolar causal dimensions: Internal-External (personalization), Stable-Unstable (permanence), Global-Specific (pervasiveness)
unverified.discrepancy: The framework's known shape is 3 bipolar continua, but the test lists 6 independent dimensions by splitting each pole into its own scale (internal/external, stable/unstable, global/specific). Poles are opposite ends of one dimension, not independent factors; measuring them separately misrepresents the ASQ shape and breaks the bipolar scoring logic.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse the 6 poles into 3 bipolar dimensions (internality, stability, globality); reverse-key one pole of each pair so each dimension runs from one pole to its opposite.

unverified.framework: Cognitive Flexibility Inventory (Dennis & Vander Wal, 2010)
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Alternatives (perceiving/generating multiple options) and Control (perceiving situations as controllable)
unverified.discrepancy: The validated CFI has 2 empirically-derived factors, but the test invents 4 (perspective, beliefUpdating, alternatives, strategySwitching). Perspective/alternatives/strategy-switching all collapse into the CFI 'Alternatives' factor, and there is no item set for the 'Control' factor.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to the CFI's 2 factors: merge perspective/alternatives/strategySwitching into 'alternatives', and add a 'control' dimension (perceived controllability / internal locus).

unverified.framework: General Self-Efficacy Scale (Schwarzer & Jerusalem) / New GSE (Chen et al.)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional: a single global self-efficacy factor (confirmed across 25-28 countries)
unverified.discrepancy: General self-efficacy is a well-established unidimensional construct, but the test splits it into 4 invented sub-factors (mastery, coping, social, agency). This is a wrongly-split unidimensional scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single unidimensional 'self-efficacy' scale; drop the 4 invented subscales and pool the items under one dimension.

unverified.framework: Snyder Hope Theory / Adult Hope Scale (AHS)
unverified.official_structure: 2 subscales: Agency (goal-directed energy) and Pathways (planning routes to goals); 'goals' is theory framing, not a scored factor.
unverified.discrepancy: Adds three factors not in Snyder's model. Agency and Pathways are correct, but 'goals', 'support', and 'meaning' are invented dimensions the recognized hope framework does not include (support/meaning belong to other constructs like social support and meaning-in-life).
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to Snyder's 2 factors (Agency + Pathways); drop 'support' and 'meaning'; fold 'goals' into intro framing rather than a scored dimension.

unverified.framework: Humility (no single canonical multidimensional scale; nearest anchors: HEXACO Honesty-Humility, Comprehensive Intellectual Humility Scale, Relational Humility Scale)
unverified.official_structure: No consensus factor structure for general humility. Competing validated instruments differ: HEXACO H-H = 4 facets (sincerity, fairness, greed-avoidance, modesty); CIHS = 4 factors; Relational Humility Scale = 3 factors.
unverified.discrepancy: The 5 dims (intellectual, fairness, modesty, accountability, teachability) are all legitimate humility facets drawn from across the literature, but do not map exactly to any one authoritative published structure, so exact-match cannot be adjudicated.

unverified.framework: Compassion Scale (Pommier, Neff & Tóth-Király, 2020)
unverified.official_structure: 4 facets: Kindness, Common Humanity, Mindfulness, and Indifference (reverse-keyed), under one general compassion factor.
unverified.discrepancy: None of the 5 dims (empathy, action, boundaries, fairness, selfCompassion) match the validated Compassion Scale facets. 'selfCompassion' is a separate construct (Neff's 6-factor Self-Compassion Scale), not part of other-directed compassion; no reverse-keyed indifference items exist.
unverified.proposed_fix: Align to Compassion Scale facets: Kindness, Common Humanity, Mindfulness, Indifference (reverse-keyed); remove self-compassion (separate construct) or split into its own test.

unverified.framework: Relationship Assessment Scale (Hendrick) / relationship satisfaction
unverified.official_structure: Relationship satisfaction as measured by the RAS (and CSI) is a strong unidimensional / single global factor.
unverified.discrepancy: Splits a validated unidimensional construct into 6 invented facets (closeness, trust, communication, repair, autonomy, future). These are relationship-quality/functioning domains, not empirically-supported satisfaction subfactors; the RAS loads on one factor.
unverified.proposed_fix: Treat as a single global satisfaction dimension (RAS/CSI are unidimensional); or relabel the test as 'relationship quality/functioning' if multidimensional facets are intended.

unverified.framework: Trust repair (organizational-behavior concept; no validated multi-factor personality instrument)
unverified.official_structure: No authoritative public factor structure exists for a 'trust repair style' self-report scale.
unverified.discrepancy: The 6 dims (accountability, consistency, transparency, empathy, boundaries, time) are reasonable trust-repair components but are not drawn from any published validated instrument, so fidelity cannot be adjudicated.

unverified.framework: Hogan Development Survey (HDS) derailers
unverified.official_structure: 11 derailer scales: Excitable, Skeptical, Cautious, Reserved, Leisurely, Bold, Mischievous, Colorful, Imaginative, Diligent, Dutiful (three clusters: moving away / against / toward).
unverified.discrepancy: Uses only 7 of the 11 HDS scales, omitting Leisurely, Mischievous, Colorful, and Dutiful. Also 'volatile' is a subscale of the Excitable scale, not the top-level scale name. Framing ('HDS-Style') is honestly approximate, but the dimension shape is incomplete relative to the framework's defining 11 derailers.
unverified.proposed_fix: Add the 4 missing derailers (Leisurely, Mischievous, Colorful, Dutiful) and rename 'volatile' to 'Excitable' to reflect all 11 HDS scales.

unverified.framework: Sparketype (Jonathan Fields)
unverified.official_structure: 10 Sparketypes: Maven, Maker, Scientist, Essentialist, Performer, Sage, Warrior, Advisor, Advocate, Nurturer
unverified.discrepancy: Only 7 dimensions; omits 4 real Sparketypes (Maven, Essentialist, Sage, Warrior) and invents a non-existent 'Solver' type (the real problem-solving type is Scientist).
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove invented 'solver' dim; add missing Maven, Essentialist, Sage, Warrior to match the 10 official Sparketypes (2 balanced items each).

unverified.framework: Karen Horney's interpersonal coping trends
unverified.official_structure: 3 coping strategies / neurotic trends: Moving Toward, Moving Against, Moving Away (as measured by the Horney-Coolidge Tridimensional Inventory)
unverified.discrepancy: Adds a 4th 'Flexible' dimension. Horney's framework is defined by 3 trends; flexible/fluid use of all three is the healthy resolution, not a separate measured type.
unverified.proposed_fix: Drop the 'flexible' dimension to leave the canonical 3 trends (toward/against/away); if kept, reframe as a balance/integration score rather than a co-equal type.

unverified.framework: Adlerian Personality Priorities (Kefir 1971; APPA)
unverified.official_structure: 4 priorities: Superiority, Control, Comfort (Avoiding), Pleasing. Four-factor structure confirmed by the Adlerian Personality Priority Assessment.
unverified.discrepancy: Adds a 5th invented dimension 'Belonging'. Belonging/social interest is an Adlerian concept but is not one of Kefir's four personality priorities. The canonical set is exactly 4.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the 'belonging' dimension and its 2 items; keep the canonical 4 priorities (Superiority, Control, Comfort, Pleasing).

unverified.framework: Emotional Contagion Scale (Doherty 1997)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional 15-item susceptibility scale (Doherty 1997); the only validated multidimensional decomposition is by emotion (happiness, love, anger, fear, sadness), not by process.
unverified.discrepancy: The four dims (Absorption, Mirroring, Broadcasting, Regulation) are invented. Worse, 'Broadcasting' (sending/spreading one's own emotions) and 'Regulation' fall outside the construct, which measures susceptibility to CATCHING others' emotions, not projecting or regulating them.
unverified.proposed_fix: Treat as a single 'susceptibility to emotional contagion' scale, or split by the 5 emotion facets (happiness, love, anger, fear, sadness); drop the out-of-construct Broadcasting and Regulation dims.

unverified.framework: Iowa-Netherlands Comparison Orientation Measure / INCOM (Gibbons & Buunk 1999)
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Ability (comparison of performance, 6 items) and Opinion (comparison of thoughts/beliefs, 5 items). Two-factor model is superior to the one-factor solution.
unverified.discrepancy: Test invents a 5-factor structure (Ability, Status, Lifestyle, Values, Progress). Only 'Ability' matches; the 'Opinion' dimension is absent and Status/Lifestyle/Progress are fabricated sub-scales the instrument does not have.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to the 2 published factors: Ability and Opinion; fold Values into Opinion and drop Status/Lifestyle/Progress.

unverified.framework: Impression Management / self-presentation (Jones & Pittman 1982; Bolino & Turnley IMS 1999)
unverified.official_structure: 5 self-presentation tactics: Self-Promotion, Ingratiation, Exemplification, Intimidation, Supplication (Bolino-Turnley IMS). Alternatively BIDR Impression Management is a unidimensional social-desirability scale.
unverified.discrepancy: Dims (Polish, Authenticity, Status, Agreeableness, Privacy) match neither recognized structure. 'Agreeableness' borrows a Big Five label, and Authenticity/Privacy are the inverse of impression management rather than tactics of it.
unverified.proposed_fix: Align dimensions to the 5 Jones-Pittman/Bolino-Turnley tactics (Self-Promotion, Ingratiation, Exemplification, Intimidation, Supplication).

unverified.framework: Reiss Motivation Profile / 16 Basic Desires (Steven Reiss)
unverified.official_structure: 16 basic desires: power, independence, curiosity, acceptance, order, saving, honor, idealism, social contact, family, status, vengeance, romance, eating, physical exercise (activity), tranquility
unverified.discrepancy: Only 10 of the 16 defining desires are present. Missing 6: family, vengeance, romance, eating, physical exercise/activity, tranquility. The whole point of the Reiss framework is exactly 16 desires, so 10 misrepresents the shape a user would learn.
unverified.proposed_fix: Add the 6 missing desires (family, vengeance, romance, eating, physical activity, tranquility) with 2 balanced items each to restore the canonical 16-desire shape.

unverified.framework: Tolerance of Ambiguity (Budner 1962 / MSTAT / Furnham)
unverified.official_structure: No consensus factor structure: Budner proposed unidimensional (CFA-unsupported, low reliability); Furnham reported 4 factors; MSTAT-II treats it as essentially unidimensional. Multiple competing, non-replicating models.
unverified.discrepancy: The 4 dims (comfort, closureNeed, exploration, threat) are plausible facets but map to no single authoritative published structure; because the construct itself lacks an agreed factor model, fidelity cannot be adjudicated. closureNeed also drifts toward the separate Need-for-Closure construct.

unverified.framework: Personal Need for Structure scale (Thompson, Naccarato, Parker & Moskowitz)
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Desire for Structure and Response to Lack of Structure (some uses treat it as unidimensional). 12 items, 4 reverse-coded.
unverified.discrepancy: Invents a 4-factor structure (routine, categorization, planning, chaosSensitivity) that does not correspond to the published 2-factor PNS model; no reverse-keyed items despite PNS using them.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to the 2 published factors: Desire for Structure (routine + categorization + planning items) and Response to Lack of Structure (chaosSensitivity items); add at least one reverse-keyed item per factor.

unverified.framework: Affect Intensity Measure, AIM (Larsen & Diener 1987)
unverified.official_structure: Originally functionally unidimensional (5 correlated factors under one second-order factor); best-supported multidimensional model = 3 factors: Positive Affectivity, Negative Intensity, Negative Reactivity (some 4-factor solutions add Serenity).
unverified.discrepancy: AIM measures the intensity/reactivity with which affect is experienced. 'positive' and 'negative' are defensible, but 'expression' (others reading your emotions) and 'recovery' (coming down from emotion) are not AIM constructs — they are invented factors that confound emotional expressivity and regulation with intensity.
unverified.proposed_fix: Replace 'expression' and 'recovery' with AIM's actual factors: keep Positive (Positive Affectivity) and Negative Intensity, and add Negative Reactivity (and optionally Serenity) instead of expression/recovery.

unverified.framework: Prosocial Personality Battery, PSB (Penner et al.)
unverified.official_structure: 2 higher-order factors (Other-Oriented Empathy; Helpfulness) built from 7 subscales: social responsibility, empathic concern, perspective taking, personal distress, other-oriented moral reasoning, mutual moral reasoning, self-reported altruism.
unverified.discrepancy: Invents a 5-factor structure (empathy, responsibility, moralConcern, practicalHelp, community). It does not match the 2-factor PSB model nor the 7 subscales; 'community' has no counterpart in the PSB. personal distress (reverse-keyed in PSB) is absent.
unverified.proposed_fix: Restructure to the 2 PSB factors: Other-Oriented Empathy (empathy + responsibility + moralConcern) and Helpfulness (practicalHelp/altruism); drop 'community' as it is not a PSB dimension, or fold into responsibility.

unverified.framework: Social Dominance Orientation, SDO7 (Ho et al. 2015)
unverified.official_structure: Overall SDO is one construct with 2 theorized subdimensions: SDO-Dominance (SDO-D) and SDO-Anti-Egalitarianism (SDO-E); 16 items, half pro-trait and half con-trait (reverse-keyed).
unverified.discrepancy: Invents a 4-factor structure (hierarchy, equality, competition, inclusion). Real SDO = 2 subfactors (Dominance, Anti-egalitarianism). 'competition' and 'inclusion' are not SDO facets; the egalitarian items (equality, inclusion) are the con-trait side of SDO but are scored as separate positive dimensions with reverse:false rather than reverse-keyed SDO items.
unverified.proposed_fix: Restructure to 2 subdimensions: SDO-D (hierarchy/dominance items) and SDO-E (anti-egalitarianism); convert 'equality' and 'inclusion' items into reverse-keyed con-trait items within those two factors instead of standalone dimensions.

unverified.framework: Personality Assessment Inventory (PAI; Morey)
unverified.official_structure: 22 non-overlapping scales: 4 validity, 11 clinical (Somatic, Anxiety, Anxiety-Related Disorders, Depression, Mania, Paranoia, Schizophrenia, Borderline, Antisocial, Alcohol, Drug), 5 treatment, 2 interpersonal. Not a 5-dimension instrument.
unverified.discrepancy: The 5 dims (stress, mood, warmth, impulse, support) are not the recognizable shape of the PAI. Stress/Nonsupport(support)/Warmth happen to be real minor treatment/interpersonal scales, but Mood and Impulse are not PAI scale names, and the whole clinical-psychopathology core (11 clinical scales) that defines the PAI is absent. Reads as an arbitrary 5-scale grab, not a PAI-shaped approximation.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either represent the PAI's recognizable clinical shape (curated subset of the 11 clinical scales, e.g. anxiety, depression, mania, paranoia, borderline) or relabel the quiz as a generic emotional-needs screen rather than a PAI-style test.

unverified.framework: Big Five Inventory-2 (BFI-2; Soto & John, 2017)
