unverified.discrepancy: Adds a 5th co-equal dimension 'adaptability'. Forté's four primary D/E/P/C traits are all present and correctly named, but adaptability is a derived flexibility concept in Forté reporting, not one of the four core drivers, so it inflates the dimension count from 4 to 5.
unverified.proposed_fix: Drop the 'adaptability' dimension; Forté's core shape is 4 traits (Dominance, Extroversion, Patience, Conformity).

unverified.framework: Swedish Universities Scales of Personality (SSP)
unverified.official_structure: 13 scales (Somatic/Psychic Trait Anxiety, Stress Susceptibility, Lack of Assertiveness, Impulsiveness, Adventure Seeking, Detachment, Social Desirability, Embitterment, Trait Irritability, Mistrust, Verbal/Physical Trait Aggression), typically grouped into 3 higher-order factors (Neuroticism, Aggressiveness, Extraversion/Disinhibition)
unverified.discrepancy: Declares only 5 dimensions (anxiety, impulsiveness, detachment, aggression, adventure). These are authentic SSP scale names/domains with no invented factors, but the test covers only ~5 of the 13 SSP scales, omitting Stress Susceptibility, Lack of Assertiveness, Embitterment, Trait Irritability, Mistrust, etc. Matches neither the 13-scale nor the 3-factor structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either align to SSP's 3 higher-order factors (Neuroticism, Aggressiveness, Extraversion) or frame explicitly as a 5-scale subset; currently it partially samples the 13-scale structure.

unverified.framework: Jackson Personality Research Form (PRF)
unverified.official_structure: 20 content scales based on Murray's needs (Abasement, Achievement, Affiliation, Aggression, Autonomy, Change, Cognitive Structure, Defendence, Dominance, Endurance, Exhibition, Harmavoidance, Impulsivity, Nurturance, Order, Play, Sentience, Social Recognition, Succorance, Understanding) plus 2 validity scales (22 total)
unverified.discrepancy: Declares only 5 dimensions (achievement, affiliation, dominance, endurance, order). All 5 are genuine PRF scales, correctly named with no invented factors, but this covers only 5 of 20 need-scales, omitting major ones (Aggression, Autonomy, Nurturance, Understanding, Impulsivity, etc.).
unverified.proposed_fix: Frame explicitly as a 5-scale sampler, or add more core PRF need-scales; the real PRF has 20 co-equal content scales and no canonical reduced 5-factor structure.

unverified.framework: Osgood Semantic Differential
unverified.official_structure: 3 cross-cultural factors (EPA): Evaluation (good-bad), Potency (strong-weak), Activity (active-passive).
unverified.discrepancy: Lists 5 dims (warmth, power, activity, order, meaning). Warmth≈Evaluation, power≈Potency, activity=Activity are faithful, but 'order' and 'meaning' are invented factors not part of Osgood's famous 3-factor EPA structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to Osgood's 3 EPA dimensions — rename warmth->Evaluation, power->Potency, keep Activity; drop invented 'order' and 'meaning'.

unverified.framework: FIRO-B — Schutz
unverified.official_structure: 3 interpersonal needs (Inclusion, Control, Affection) each crossed with 2 behavioral modes (Expressed, Wanted) = 6 scales (Expressed/Wanted Inclusion, Control, Affection).
unverified.discrepancy: Lists inclusion, control, affection, expressed, wanted as 5 parallel flat dimensions. This mis-represents the 3x2 crossing: 'expressed' and 'wanted' are modes applied to each need, not standalone dimensions alongside them. The correct structure is 6 scales, not 5 flat dims.
unverified.proposed_fix: Replace the 5 flat dims with the 6 FIRO-B scales formed by crossing Inclusion/Control/Affection x Expressed/Wanted (e.g. expressed-inclusion, wanted-inclusion, ... wanted-affection), with balanced items per cell.

unverified.framework: Interpersonal Adjective Scales (IAS) — Wiggins interpersonal circumplex
unverified.official_structure: 8 octants of the interpersonal circumplex: Assured-Dominant (PA), Arrogant-Calculating (BC), Cold-hearted (DE), Aloof-Introverted (FG), Unassured-Submissive (HI), Unassuming-Ingenuous (JK), Warm-Agreeable (LM), Gregarious-Extraverted (NO).
unverified.discrepancy: Lists only 6 dims (dominant, warm, modest/submissive, reserved/cold, firm/hostile, nurturing). The IAS circumplex is defined by exactly 8 octants; a 6-segment version breaks the octant structure and omits blends (e.g. arrogant-calculating, gregarious-extraverted).
unverified.proposed_fix: Expand to the 8 circumplex octants (PA/BC/DE/FG/HI/JK/LM/NO) with balanced items each; remove ad-hoc labels like 'firm/hostile'.

unverified.framework: Leary Interpersonal Check List (ICL) — interpersonal circumplex
unverified.official_structure: 8-octant interpersonal circumplex (16 subcategories collapsed to 8 octants): Managerial-Autocratic, Competitive-Narcissistic, Aggressive-Sadistic, Rebellious-Distrustful, Self-effacing-Masochistic, Docile-Dependent, Cooperative-Overconventional, Responsible-Hypernormal.
unverified.discrepancy: Lists only 6 dims (managerial, competitive, distrustful, withdrawn, compliant, nurturant). The ICL's defining shape is an 8-octant circumplex; 6 octants is the wrong framework shape (missing the aggressive/hostile and responsible/overconventional octants as distinct segments).
unverified.proposed_fix: Expand to the 8 ICL octants (add an aggressive-sadistic and a responsible-hypernormal/cooperative-overconventional octant) so the circumplex has its standard 8 segments.

unverified.framework: Circumplex Scales of Interpersonal Values (CSIV) — Locke (2000)
unverified.official_structure: 64-item measure with 8 octant scales mapping the interpersonal circumplex (agentic/communal blends), e.g. values around being in control, being close/warm, being independent, being cooperative/harmonious, being admired, being deferred to, etc. — 8 octants.
unverified.discrepancy: Lists only 6 dims (influence, warmth, independence, harmony, recognition, support). CSIV is explicitly an 8-octant circumplex measure; 6 dims omits two octants and breaks the circumplex structure required for a Tier A match.
unverified.proposed_fix: Expand to the 8 CSIV octants of the interpersonal circumplex (agency+/-, communion+/- and their four blends) with balanced items per octant.

unverified.framework: Snyder Self-Monitoring Scale
unverified.official_structure: Snyder & Gangestad argue for a unitary construct (single self-monitoring score); the only recognized multifactor solution is 3 factors (Acting, Extraversion, Other-Directedness; Briggs, Cheek & Buss 1980). Lennox & Wolfe's revision = 2 factors.
unverified.discrepancy: Invents 5 dimensions (adaptation, expressiveness, cue, authenticity, strategy) that match no published factor structure. Also all items reverse:false, though the real scale is heavily reverse-keyed and 'authenticity' items are conceptually reverse-scored.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single 'self-monitoring' dimension, or use the 3 Briggs et al. factors (Acting/Public Performing, Extraversion, Other-Directedness); reverse-key the low-self-monitoring ('authenticity') items.

unverified.framework: Marlowe-Crowne Social Desirability Scale (MC-SDS)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional: a single social-desirability score (33 items). The only replicated split is 2 factors (Attribution/self-deceptive enhancement vs Denial/impression management; Millham, Ramanaiah).
unverified.discrepancy: Invents 5 dimensions (approval, virtue, control, honesty, norms) with no basis in the instrument's structure. 'honesty' items are conceptually reverse-keyed but marked reverse:false.
unverified.proposed_fix: Use a single 'social-desirability' dimension (or at most 2: Attribution vs Denial); reverse-key the frank/honest self-admission items.

unverified.framework: Future Time Perspective scale (Carstensen & Lang)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional (single 10-item FTP score) or 2 factors (Focus on Opportunities, Focus on Limitations; Cate & John 2007); some report 3 (remaining opportunities, extended future/remaining time, limitations).
unverified.discrepancy: Has 5 dimensions; only opportunity and limitation map to real poles. planning, legacy, and urgency are invented factors not in the FTP structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to the 2 published poles: 'opportunity' (Focus on Opportunities) and 'limitation' (Focus on Limitations); drop planning, legacy, urgency.

unverified.framework: Consideration of Future Consequences (CFC) scale
unverified.official_structure: Originally unidimensional (Strathman et al. 1994, 12 items); the accepted refinement is 2 factors: CFC-Immediate and CFC-Future (Joireman, Shaffer, Balliet & Strathman 2012).
unverified.discrepancy: Splits the scale into 5 life-content domains (health, money, relationships, ethics, environment). These are topical domains, not the CFC factor structure, which concerns time horizon (immediate vs future) not life areas.
unverified.proposed_fix: Replace the 5 domain dimensions with the 2 real factors: 'CFC-Immediate' (weighting immediate outcomes) and 'CFC-Future' (weighting distant outcomes), reverse-keying the immediate items.

unverified.framework: Schwartz Maximization Scale (MS)
unverified.official_structure: 3 subscales: High Standards, Alternative Search, Decision Difficulty (Schwartz et al. 2002; confirmed by Nenkov et al. 2008). Regret is a SEPARATE scale (Schwartz's Regret Scale), not a maximization subscale.
unverified.discrepancy: Has 5 dimensions (maximizing, satisficing, regret, comparison, search). Only 'search' maps to a real subscale (Alternative Search). regret belongs to a distinct scale; maximizing, satisficing, comparison are invented and Decision Difficulty + High Standards are missing.
unverified.proposed_fix: Use the 3 official subscales: 'high-standards', 'alternative-search', 'decision-difficulty'; remove regret (separate scale), satisficing, comparison, maximizing.

unverified.framework: Affective temperament model (TEMPS)
unverified.official_structure: 5 affective temperaments: Depressive, Cyclothymic, Hyperthymic, Irritable, Anxious
unverified.discrepancy: As a Tier A instrument, dimensions must match the published affective-temperament (TEMPS) factor structure exactly. Instead it invents 5 non-canonical dims (steady, sensitive, intense, upbeat, reactive) that do not correspond to the established temperaments.
unverified.proposed_fix: Replace the 5 invented dims with the canonical TEMPS temperaments: depressive, cyclothymic, hyperthymic, irritable, anxious (2 items each).

unverified.framework: ASEBA Adult Self-Report (ASR)
unverified.official_structure: 8 empirically-based syndrome scales: Anxious/Depressed, Withdrawn, Somatic Complaints, Thought Problems, Attention Problems, Aggressive Behavior, Rule-breaking Behavior, Intrusive
unverified.discrepancy: The known ASR shape is 8 syndrome scales; the test reinvents these as 5 generic themes (attention, emotion, relationships, rules, coping) that do not map to the ASEBA structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Align dims to the ASR syndrome scales, e.g. anxious/depressed, withdrawn, somatic, attention, aggressive/rule-breaking, thought-problems; drop the invented 'coping' theme.

unverified.framework: SCL-90-R (Symptom Checklist-90-Revised)
unverified.official_structure: 9 primary symptom dimensions: Somatization, Obsessive-Compulsive, Interpersonal Sensitivity, Depression, Anxiety, Hostility, Phobic Anxiety, Paranoid Ideation, Psychoticism
unverified.discrepancy: Only 6 of the 9 defining dimensions are present (somatic, obsessive, sensitive, mood, anxiety, hostility). Missing Phobic Anxiety, Paranoid Ideation, and Psychoticism — the SCL-90 is defined by its 9-dimension shape.
unverified.proposed_fix: Add the 3 missing dimensions (phobic anxiety, paranoid ideation, psychoticism) to reflect the full 9-dimension SCL-90-R shape.

unverified.framework: Brief Symptom Inventory (BSI)
unverified.official_structure: 9 symptom dimensions (same as SCL-90-R): Somatization, Obsessive-Compulsive, Interpersonal Sensitivity, Depression, Anxiety, Hostility, Phobic Anxiety, Paranoid Ideation, Psychoticism
unverified.discrepancy: Has 6 dims, one of which ('coping') is not a BSI dimension at all. Missing Obsessive-Compulsive, Phobic Anxiety, Paranoid Ideation, and Psychoticism; the BSI shares SCL-90's 9-dimension shape.
unverified.proposed_fix: Drop invented 'coping'; align to the 9 BSI dimensions (add obsessive-compulsive, phobic anxiety, paranoid ideation, psychoticism).

unverified.framework: DASS / DASS-21 (Depression Anxiety Stress Scales)
unverified.official_structure: Exactly 3 scales: Depression, Anxiety, Stress
unverified.discrepancy: The DASS is defined by exactly 3 subscales. The test appends two invented dimensions ('coping', 'support') that are not part of the instrument, distorting the 3-factor shape into 5.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the 'coping' and 'support' dimensions; DASS = 3 subscales only (depression, anxiety, stress). (Note: DASS is a free public instrument and arguably belongs in Tier A.)

unverified.framework: Psychological Capital Questionnaire (PCQ), Luthans
unverified.official_structure: PsyCap is exactly 4 dimensions, the 'HERO' model: Hope, Efficacy, Resilience, Optimism. There is no fifth factor.
unverified.discrepancy: Adds an invented 5th dimension 'Support'; PsyCap is definitionally the 4-factor HERO model. The framework is universally known for exactly 4 components.
unverified.proposed_fix: Remove the 'support' dimension (and its 2 items); keep only hope, efficacy, resilience, optimism to match the 4-factor HERO structure.

unverified.framework: Short Grit Scale (Grit-S), Duckworth & Quinn 2009
unverified.official_structure: Grit-S (and Grit-O) is a validated 2-factor scale: Consistency of Interest and Perseverance of Effort.
unverified.discrepancy: Uses 5 dimensions (perseverance, consistency, recovery, discipline, purpose). Only 'perseverance' and 'consistency' are real Grit factors; recovery, discipline, and purpose are invented, splitting a published 2-factor instrument.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to the 2 published factors: fold 'recovery' + 'discipline' into 'perseverance-of-effort' and 'purpose' into 'consistency-of-interest' (or drop). Rename to Consistency of Interest / Perseverance of Effort.

unverified.framework: Work Values (O*NET / Theory of Work Adjustment six work values)
unverified.official_structure: The O*NET/TWA six work values are: Achievement, Independence, Recognition, Relationships, Support, Working Conditions. (Super's older WVI has 15/12 values.)
unverified.discrepancy: Five dims match O*NET exactly (achievement, autonomy=Independence, recognition, relationships, conditions=Working Conditions) but the 6th value 'Support' is replaced with the invented 'Security', so the published 6-value set is not matched exactly.
unverified.proposed_fix: Rename 'security' -> 'support' and reframe its 2 items around supportive management/company backing to match O*NET's sixth work value.

unverified.framework: Diener Flourishing Scale (2009)
unverified.official_structure: 8 items, unidimensional — a single overall 'flourishing' score with NO subscales
unverified.discrepancy: The real Flourishing Scale is a single-factor summary measure; this test invents a 6-subscale structure (meaning, relationships, competence, engagement, optimism, respect) that does not exist in the instrument. Wrongly splits a unidimensional scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse the 6 invented subscales into one overall 'flourishing' dimension (the Flourishing Scale is unidimensional); or, if multi-dimensional wellbeing is intended, relabel as a PERMA-style test rather than Flourishing Scale.

unverified.framework: Subjective Happiness Scale (Lyubomirsky & Lepper, 1999)
unverified.official_structure: 4 items, unidimensional — a single subjective-happiness composite score with NO subscales
unverified.discrepancy: SHS is a single-factor 4-item scale; this test fabricates a 5-subscale structure (overall, comparison, contentment, uplift, recovery). Wrongly splits a unidimensional scale into invented factors.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single 'subjective happiness' dimension (SHS is unidimensional, 4 items).

unverified.framework: Self-Determination Theory (basic psychological needs)
unverified.official_structure: 3 basic psychological needs: Autonomy, Competence, Relatedness (the intrinsic/integrated labels belong to a separate SDT sub-theory, the OIT motivation-regulation continuum)
unverified.discrepancy: The test uses 5 dims — the 3 correct needs plus 'intrinsic' and 'integrated', which are motivation-regulation types from a different SDT construct (OIT), conflating two distinct sub-theories into one scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Restrict to the 3 basic needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness); drop 'intrinsic' and 'integrated' (they are OIT regulation types, not needs), or re-scope the test explicitly as a motivation-continuum measure.

unverified.framework: Basic Empathy Scale (Jolliffe & Farrington, 2006)
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Cognitive empathy and Affective empathy
unverified.discrepancy: The BES has exactly 2 factors (cognitive, affective); this test adds 3 invented subscales (reading, compassion, boundaries) not part of the instrument.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to 2 factors (cognitive, affective); fold or drop reading, compassion, boundaries.

unverified.framework: Toronto Alexithymia Scale (TAS-20)
unverified.official_structure: 3 factors: Difficulty Identifying Feelings (DIF), Difficulty Describing Feelings (DDF), Externally-Oriented Thinking (EOT)
unverified.discrepancy: The TAS-20 has 3 subscales; this test keeps the 3 correct ones (identify, describe, external) but adds 2 invented factors ('body' and 'imagination') that are not part of the TAS-20 factor structure.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to the 3 TAS-20 factors (identify/DIF, describe/DDF, external/EOT); drop the 'body' and 'imagination' subscales.

unverified.framework: Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (Gross & John, 2003)
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Cognitive Reappraisal and Expressive Suppression
unverified.discrepancy: The ERQ has exactly 2 subscales; this test keeps the 2 correct ones (reappraisal, suppression) but adds 3 invented factors (expression, acceptance, recovery) not in the instrument.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to 2 factors (reappraisal, suppression); drop expression, acceptance, recovery.

unverified.framework: Ruminative Responses Scale (RRS), Response Styles Questionnaire; refined by Treynor, Gonzalez & Nolen-Hoeksema (2003)
unverified.official_structure: 2 factors: Brooding and Reflection (Treynor et al. 2003 refined RRS). The original construct is a single rumination response style decomposed into these two subscales.
unverified.discrepancy: Uses 6 dimensions (brooding, reflection, replay, looping, criticism, interrupt). Brooding and Reflection are correct, but replay/looping/criticism/interrupt are invented facets not in the RRS; 'interrupt' is a positively-framed coping dimension foreign to the scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to 2 dims (Brooding, Reflection); fold replay/looping/criticism items into Brooding and drop the invented 'interrupt' dimension.

unverified.framework: Self-Consciousness Scale (Fenigstein, Scheier & Buss, 1975)
unverified.official_structure: 3 subscales: Private Self-Consciousness, Public Self-Consciousness, and Social Anxiety.
unverified.discrepancy: Uses 5 dimensions (private, public, evaluation, reflection, monitoring). Private and Public are correct, but the third canonical subscale (Social Anxiety) is missing, and evaluation/reflection/monitoring are invented.
unverified.proposed_fix: Use the 3 official subscales: Private Self-Consciousness, Public Self-Consciousness, Social Anxiety; drop invented evaluation/reflection/monitoring.

unverified.framework: Private Self-Consciousness subscale of the Self-Consciousness Scale (Fenigstein, Scheier & Buss, 1975)
unverified.official_structure: A single subscale; at most decomposed into 2 sub-facets in later work (Internal State Awareness and Self-Reflectiveness). Not a 5-facet construct.
unverified.discrepancy: Splits a unidimensional subscale into 5 invented facets (feelings, motives, values, identity, dialogue), none of which are recognized subdimensions.
unverified.proposed_fix: Treat as a single Private Self-Consciousness dimension (or at most the 2 documented facets: Internal State Awareness, Self-Reflectiveness); remove the 5 invented facets.

unverified.framework: Public Self-Consciousness subscale of the Self-Consciousness Scale (Fenigstein, Scheier & Buss, 1975)
unverified.official_structure: A single unidimensional subscale (awareness of the self as viewed by others), ~7 items, one factor.
unverified.discrepancy: Splits a unidimensional subscale into 5 invented facets (impression, appearance, performance, cues, flexibility). 'Flexibility' resembles self-monitoring, a different construct.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single Public Self-Consciousness dimension; remove invented facets (esp. 'flexibility', which belongs to Self-Monitoring).

unverified.framework: Fear of Negative Evaluation Scale (Watson & Friend, 1969; Brief FNE, Leary, 1983)
unverified.official_structure: A single unidimensional construct (fear of negative evaluation) with high internal consistency; the brief FNE at most shows a method artifact split between straight- and reverse-worded items, not distinct content factors.
unverified.discrepancy: Splits a unidimensional scale into 6 invented facets (criticism, rejection, performance, uncertainty, reassurance, recovery). 'Recovery' is a positively-framed coping dimension foreign to the scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single Fear of Negative Evaluation dimension; drop invented 'recovery'/'reassurance' coping facets.

unverified.framework: Interaction Anxiousness Scale (Leary, 1983)
unverified.official_structure: A single unidimensional 15-item scale measuring dispositional social interaction anxiety (one factor).
unverified.discrepancy: Splits a unidimensional scale into 6 invented facets (initiation, flow, authority, groups, intimacy, recovery); the IAS does not define situational subscales, and 'recovery' is an invented positive dimension.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single Interaction Anxiousness dimension; remove invented situational facets and 'recovery'.

unverified.framework: Social Avoidance and Distress Scale (SADS; Watson & Friend, 1969)
unverified.official_structure: 2 components: Social Avoidance and Social Distress (originally a single total score, factor-analytically two factors).
unverified.discrepancy: Uses 6 dimensions (anticipation, avoidance, distress, unfamiliar, safety, reconnect). Avoidance and Distress are correct, but anticipation/unfamiliar/safety/reconnect are invented; 'safety' and 'reconnect' are coping constructs foreign to the scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reduce to 2 dims (Social Avoidance, Social Distress); fold anticipation/unfamiliar items in and drop invented safety/reconnect coping facets.

unverified.framework: UCLA Loneliness Scale (Russell, Peplau & Ferguson, 1978; R-UCLA, Russell, 1996)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional — a single global loneliness construct (the most commonly used unidimensional loneliness scale).
unverified.discrepancy: Splits a unidimensional scale into 6 invented facets (belonging, closeness, network, isolation, disconnect, reconnect). If a multidimensional model were intended it would be a different instrument (De Jong Gierveld / SELSA), not UCLA-style.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single global Loneliness dimension (UCLA is unidimensional); if multidimensionality is wanted, re-badge as a De Jong Gierveld-style test with its 2 factors (emotional, social).

unverified.framework: Adjective Check List (ACL; Gough & Heilbrun)
unverified.official_structure: 300-adjective checklist scored on 37 scales across 5 categories: modus operandi (4), need (15, per Murray), topical (9), transactional analysis (5), origence-intellectence (4)
unverified.discrepancy: Uses 6 invented trait buckets (drive, warmth, order, sensitivity, creativity, confidence) that correspond to none of the ACL's 37 scales or 5 scale categories; the ACL's defining shape (adjective-endorsement scored across need/topical/ego/origence scales) is absent.
unverified.proposed_fix: Reframe dims to reflect real ACL scale families (e.g. Achievement, Nurturance/Affiliation, Order, Self-control, Personal Adjustment, Self-confidence) or relabel as a generic adjective-based Big-Five-style test; current 6 dims do not represent the ACL's 37-scale structure.

unverified.framework: Q-sort method / California Q-Set (Block)
unverified.official_structure: Q-sort is a rank-ordering METHOD (e.g. 100-item California Q-Set forced into a fixed distribution), not a fixed-factor instrument; classic use compares real-self vs ideal-self (Rogers). No canonical dimensional structure exists.
unverified.discrepancy: The 6 dims (real, ideal, social, strengths, conflicts, change) are an invented framing; Q-sort has no authoritative factor structure to match or violate, though the real/ideal-self emphasis loosely reflects its classic Rogerian use.

unverified.framework: Acceptance and Action Questionnaire-II (AAQ-II; Bond et al., 2011)
unverified.official_structure: Single-factor (unidimensional) 7-item measure of psychological inflexibility / experiential avoidance
unverified.discrepancy: Splits a validated unidimensional scale into 6 dims (acceptance, values, action, fusion, avoidance, present) — these are the six ACT hexaflex processes, i.e. the therapy model, not the AAQ questionnaire's structure. Wrongly-split unidimensional scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single dimension 'psychological inflexibility / experiential avoidance' (all items keyed to that one construct); the AAQ-II is unidimensional. If a 6-process breakdown is desired, relabel the test as an ACT hexaflex / psychological-flexibility style test rather than AAQ.

unverified.framework: Social Connectedness Scale / SCS-R (Lee & Robbins, 1995; 2001)
unverified.official_structure: Unidimensional – a single general 'social connectedness' factor (all items load on one belongingness dimension; SCS-R items are a single scale).
unverified.discrepancy: The real SCS is a single-factor scale, but this test splits it into 6 invented facets (belonging, closeness, inclusion, continuity, mutuality, repair) that the instrument does not define. This is a wrongly-split unidimensional scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to one 'social connectedness' dimension (SCS/SCS-R is unidimensional); if facets are desired, present them as descriptive sub-themes rather than as the scale's factor structure.

unverified.framework: Need to Belong Scale (Leary, Kelly, Cottrell & Schreindorfer, 2013)
unverified.official_structure: Essentially unidimensional – a single 'need to belong' factor across 10 items (some studies report a minor 2-factor split, but the intended construct is one general need).
unverified.discrepancy: The NTBS is a single-factor measure, but this test invents 6 facets (inclusion, acceptance, rejection, identity, attachment, independence). 'Independence/autonomy' is conceptually opposite to the need-to-belong construct and does not belong in the scale.
unverified.proposed_fix: Collapse to a single 'need to belong' dimension (10-item scale is unidimensional); at minimum drop the contradictory 'independence' facet, which measures the opposite pole.

unverified.framework: Multidimensional Scale of Perceived Social Support / MSPSS (Zimet et al., 1988)
unverified.official_structure: Three source-based subscales: Family, Friends, and Significant Other (4 items each).
unverified.discrepancy: MSPSS is defined by 3 SOURCE dimensions. This test keeps family/friends/partner but adds 3 support-TYPE dimensions (practical, emotional, access) that MSPSS does not have – mixing a source taxonomy with an invented content taxonomy.
unverified.proposed_fix: Use exactly the 3 MSPSS source dimensions – Family, Friends, Significant Other (Close Other) – and remove practical, emotional, and access; the framework is source-based, not support-type-based.

unverified.framework: Interpersonal Trust Scale (Rotter, 1967)
unverified.official_structure: A generalized-expectancy measure scored primarily as a single total interpersonal-trust score (Rotter treated it as one dimension; no canonical reliability/honesty/benevolence/vulnerability/skepticism/repair subscales).
unverified.discrepancy: The 6 dimensions (reliability, honesty, benevolence, vulnerability, skepticism, repair) do not correspond to Rotter's ITS; they resemble modern organizational-trust models (e.g., Mayer et al.'s ability/benevolence/integrity), not the generalized-trust construct the ITS is known for.
unverified.proposed_fix: Either present as a single generalized-trust score (faithful to Rotter's ITS) or relabel the test to the trust framework it actually reflects (e.g., an ability/benevolence/integrity trust model) rather than Rotter's ITS.
